note_data.title: Books of prime entry
keyPoints: **Books of prime entry** are records where transactions are first recorded from source documents before posting to the ledger
**Sales day book** records all credit sales transactions from sales invoices
**Purchases day book** records all credit purchases transactions from purchase invoices
**Sales returns day book** records all goods returned by customers using credit notes issued
**Purchases returns day book** records all goods returned to suppliers using credit notes received
**Cash book** records all cash and bank transactions and serves as both a book of prime entry and part of the ledger
**Petty cash book** records small cash payments using the imprest system
**General journal** records transactions that cannot be recorded in other books of prime entry such as opening entries, closing entries, and correction of errors
Source documents provide evidence for transactions: invoices for sales/purchases, credit notes for returns, receipts for cash transactions

definitions.definition: A record where business transactions are first entered from source documents before being posted to the ledger accounts
A book of prime entry used to record specific types of transactions such as credit sales or credit purchases
A book of prime entry used to record transactions that cannot be entered in any other book of prime entry
An original document that provides evidence of a business transaction
The process of transferring entries from books of prime entry to the ledger accounts

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Name the specific book of prime entry without explanation
Give characteristics or features of the book of prime entry

modelAnswer.question: Explain why businesses use books of prime entry. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Books of prime entry provide a chronological record of transactions which makes it easier to trace transactions back to source documents. They reduce errors because transactions are recorded systematically from source documents before posting to ledger accounts. They save time because similar transactions are grouped together making the posting process more efficient.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Chronological record/easier to trace transactions

commonMistakes: Never say books of prime entry are 'part of the ledger' — they are separate records that feed into the ledger
Students often confuse day books with ledger accounts — day books record transactions, ledger accounts show balances
Don't write 'cash book records cash' — specify it records cash receipts AND cash payments
Never say journal records 'all transactions' — it only records transactions that cannot go in other books of prime entry
Students forget that books of prime entry use source documents — always mention the link to source documents
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 1 multiple choice and Paper 2 structured questions
Always specify whether transactions are 'credit' sales/purchases when describing day books
Learn the specific source documents for each book of prime entry
Practice identifying which book of prime entry to use for different transactions
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Flow diagram showing source documents → books of prime entry → ledger accounts, with examples of each book (sales day book, purchases day book, cash book, journal) and their corresponding source documents
